mappings.rationaleAI: Angkor Wat was built for Suryavarman II (r. 1113-c. 1150) during the height of the Khmer state, directly covering the establishment of Angkor, Hindu and Buddhist religious traditions in Cambodian architecture, and the temple's 900-year role as a centre of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Animism reflecting the religious transformations outlined in B.3.c through B.3.e.

Zheng He's seven expeditions (1405-1433) sailed extensively through Southeast Asia, establishing tributary relationships with regional states and facilitating trade in spices and porcelain, directly relevant to East Asian influences on SE Asian civilizations and the maritime trade networks connecting China with the Indonesian Archipelago.

The VOC's displacement of the Portuguese from key trading posts in the Indonesian archipelago and its monopoly over nutmeg, mace, cloves, and cinnamon provide essential context for European intrusions into Malaya following the Portuguese conquest of Malacca (1511) and the spice trade that defined the Indonesian Archipelago's engagement with the wider world.

Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism and its propagation throughout his Mauryan empire initiated the transmission of Indian religious and cultural influences to Southeast Asia, foundational to understanding the Indianization of Myanmar (B.1.a) and Cambodia (B.3.a) where Theravada Buddhism became central to state and society.
